title: Acute Rhinosinusitis
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Primary Care, Emergency Medicine, ENT, Infectious Disease
objectives: Distinguish viral from bacterial acute rhinosinusitis clinically
Apply IDSA diagnostic criteria for acute bacterial rhinosinusitis (ABRS)
Understand when watchful waiting vs antibiotics is appropriate
Select appropriate empiric antibiotic therapy per IDSA guidelines

guidelineReferences.name: IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline for ABRS 2012
keyPoints: Three clinical presentations suggest bacterial infection
Imaging not recommended for uncomplicated cases

vignette.text:
A 38-year-old woman presents with 12 days of nasal congestion, purulent nasal discharge, facial pressure/pain over the maxillary sinuses, and decreased smell. She initially had a URI with clear rhinorrhea and sore throat that seemed to improve after 5 days, but then worsened 3 days ago with thick yellow-green nasal discharge and low-grade fever.

Past Medical History: Seasonal allergies, no chronic sinus disease

Medications: Loratadine PRN

Allergies: Penicillin (childhood rash, mother unsure of details)

HEENT.nose: Erythematous, edematous turbinates; thick purulent discharge bilaterally
HEENT.face: Tenderness to palpation over bilateral maxillary sinuses
HEENT.eyes: No periorbital edema or erythema, PERRL, EOM intact
HEENT.throat: Mild posterior pharyngeal erythema, post-nasal drip

physicalExam.neuro: Alert, oriented, no focal deficits, no meningismus

diagnostics.note: Imaging not routinely indicated for uncomplicated acute rhinosinusitis

questions.stem: This patient has had symptoms for 12 days with worsening after initial improvement.
questions.question: How do you distinguish viral from bacterial acute rhinosinusitis?

options.text: 90-98% of acute rhinosinusitis is VIRAL; IDSA criteria for BACTERIAL (ABRS) - any ONE of: (1) PERSISTENT: Symptoms ≥10 days without improvement; (2) SEVERE: High fever ≥39°C (102°F) + purulent discharge + facial pain for ≥3-4 consecutive days at illness onset; (3) DOUBLE WORSENING: Initial improvement followed by new fever, headache, or increased nasal discharge after 5-6 days; This patient: Double worsening pattern → likely BACTERIAL
Purulent discharge alone indicates bacterial infection

questions.explanation: Distinguishing viral from bacterial: KEY FACT: 90-98% of acute rhinosinusitis is VIRAL. Only 0.5-2% of adult viral URIs develop secondary bacterial infection. Antibiotics are NOT effective for viral rhinosinusitis! IDSA CRITERIA FOR ABRS (need ONE): (1) PERSISTENT: Symptoms/signs ≥10 days without evidence of improvement. (2) SEVERE ONSET: High fever (≥39°C/102°F) AND purulent nasal discharge or facial pain lasting ≥3-4 consecutive days at the BEGINNING of illness. (3) DOUBLE WORSENING (Double-Sickening): New onset of fever, headache, or increased nasal discharge following a typical viral URI that lasted 5-6 days and initially seemed to be improving. This patient: Had URI that improved → then worsened at day 9-10 with new fever and purulent discharge = DOUBLE WORSENING PATTERN → suggests BACTERIAL infection.

questions.stem: You want to understand the likely pathogens.
questions.question: What are the common pathogens in acute bacterial rhinosinusitis?

options.text: Most common bacterial pathogens: STREPTOCOCCUS PNEUMONIAE (30-40%); HAEMOPHILUS INFLUENZAE (20-35%) - often beta-lactamase producing; MORAXELLA CATARRHALIS (10-15%) - nearly all beta-lactamase producing; Less common: S. aureus, anaerobes, gram-negatives (consider in nosocomial); Note: Pneumococcal resistance patterns have changed with widespread vaccination (PCV13); Beta-lactamase production drives preference for amoxicillin-clavulanate
MRSA is the most common pathogen

questions.explanation: ABRS Microbiology: PRIMARY PATHOGENS: STREPTOCOCCUS PNEUMONIAE: 30-40% of cases, Most common overall, Resistance patterns changed with PCV13 vaccination. HAEMOPHILUS INFLUENZAE: 20-35% of cases, Often non-typeable strains, 30-40% produce beta-lactamase (resist amoxicillin alone). MORAXELLA CATARRHALIS: 10-15% of cases, Nearly 100% produce beta-lactamase. LESS COMMON: S. aureus (more common in chronic/nosocomial), Streptococcus pyogenes, Anaerobes (in dental-related sinusitis), Gram-negative bacilli (nosocomial, immunocompromised). CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS: High rates of beta-lactamase production → Amoxicillin-clavulanate preferred over amoxicillin alone. Routine MRSA coverage NOT recommended for uncomplicated ABRS. Culture rarely needed unless treatment failure or complications.

questions.stem: You're considering whether imaging is needed.
questions.question: What is the role of imaging in acute rhinosinusitis?

options.text: CT sinus required for all sinusitis cases

IMAGING NOT RECOMMENDED for uncomplicated ABRS; Reasons: Abnormal imaging in MOST viral URIs (not specific); Clinical criteria sufficient for diagnosis; Added cost without improving outcomes; WHEN TO IMAGE: Suspected complication (orbital, intracranial); Recurrent episodes requiring workup; Concern for alternative diagnosis (tumor, fungal); If needed: CT with contrast (axial + coronal) preferred over plain films or MRI; Endoscopy preferred over nasopharyngeal swab for cultures

questions.explanation: Imaging in acute rhinosinusitis: NOT RECOMMENDED FOR UNCOMPLICATED ABRS: Why? Most patients with viral URI have abnormal sinus imaging! Mucosal thickening, air-fluid levels common in viral infection. Adding imaging doesn't change management. Increases cost without benefit. IDSA/AAO-HNS: No imaging for patients meeting clinical criteria unless complication suspected. WHEN IMAGING IS INDICATED: Suspected complications (orbital cellulitis, intracranial extension), Recurrent acute rhinosinusitis (≥4 episodes/year) - investigate anatomy, Alternative diagnosis suspected (tumor, fungal sinusitis), Failed treatment requiring surgical planning. IMAGING MODALITY: CT with contrast (axial AND coronal views) - preferred for complications. Plain films - less reliable, generally not recommended. MRI - reserved for suspected fungal infection or tumor, better soft tissue detail. CULTURE: Direct sinus aspiration is gold standard (rarely done). Endoscopically-guided middle meatus cultures acceptable in adults (not validated in children). Nasopharyngeal swabs NOT reliable.

questions.stem: You've diagnosed this patient with ABRS.
questions.question: When should antibiotics be prescribed for acute rhinosinusitis?

options.text: IDSA: Initiate antibiotics when ABRS criteria met; AAO-HNS: Watchful waiting (7 days) OR antibiotics - EITHER acceptable for uncomplicated ABRS; Factors favoring IMMEDIATE antibiotics: Severe symptoms (high fever, facial pain); Double worsening pattern; Immunocompromised; Comorbidities affecting recovery; Patient preference after shared decision-making; WATCHFUL WAITING: Symptomatic treatment, close follow-up, safety net prescription acceptable

questions.explanation: Treatment decision in ABRS: TWO ACCEPTABLE APPROACHES: IDSA (2012): Recommends initiating empiric antibiotics when clinical criteria for ABRS are met. Rationale: Studies show benefit when stringent criteria used. AAO-HNS (2015): EITHER watchful waiting (observation + symptomatic treatment for 7 days) OR antibiotics is acceptable for uncomplicated ABRS. Shared decision-making with patient. FACTORS FAVORING IMMEDIATE ANTIBIOTICS: Severe presentation (high fever ≥39°C, significant facial pain), Double-worsening pattern (this patient), Immunocompromised host, Significant comorbidities, Patient preference, Poor access to follow-up. WATCHFUL WAITING APPROACH: Appropriate for mild-moderate symptoms. Symptomatic treatment (see next question). Close follow-up in 7 days. Safety-net antibiotic prescription (fill if no improvement). This patient: Double-worsening + fever → reasonable to start antibiotics.

questions.stem: This patient has a reported penicillin allergy (childhood rash).
questions.question: What is the recommended empiric antibiotic therapy for ABRS?

options.text: IDSA First-line: AMOXICILLIN-CLAVULANATE (preferred over amoxicillin alone); Standard dose: 500/125 mg TID or 875/125 mg BID; High-dose: 2g/125mg BID (if risk factors for resistance); Duration: 5-7 days adults, 10-14 days children; PENICILLIN ALLERGY: Doxycycline 100mg BID, OR Respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin, moxifloxacin); AVOID: Macrolides (high S. pneumoniae resistance), TMP-SMX (high resistance rates)
Fluoroquinolones are first-line for all patients

questions.explanation: Antibiotic selection for ABRS: FIRST-LINE: AMOXICILLIN-CLAVULANATE: Preferred over amoxicillin alone (IDSA). Covers beta-lactamase producing H. influenzae and M. catarrhalis. Standard dose: 500/125 mg TID or 875/125 mg BID. HIGH-DOSE amoxicillin-clavulanate (2g/125mg BID) for: High endemic penicillin-resistant S. pneumoniae (≥10%), Recent antibiotic use (within 1 month), Age <2 or >65 years, Recent hospitalization, Immunocompromised, Daycare attendance, Severe infection. DURATION: Adults: 5-7 days. Children: 10-14 days. PENICILLIN ALLERGY: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 5-7 days (adults, not children <8). OR Respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin 500 mg daily or moxifloxacin 400 mg daily). AVOID: MACROLIDES (azithromycin, clarithromycin): 30-40% S. pneumoniae resistance. TMP-SMX: High resistance rates. These are NOT recommended for empiric ABRS therapy. This patient: Childhood rash → likely low-risk allergy. Could consider amoxicillin-clavulanate if rash was non-severe, or use doxycycline.

questions.stem: You want to provide symptomatic relief.
questions.question: What adjunctive treatments are recommended for acute rhinosinusitis?

options.text: RECOMMENDED: Saline nasal irrigation (physiologic or hypertonic) - improves mucociliary clearance; Intranasal corticosteroids - reduce inflammation, may speed recovery; Analgesics/antipyretics (NSAIDs, acetaminophen); NOT RECOMMENDED/LIMITED BENEFIT: Oral antihistamines (dry secretions, no proven benefit); Oral decongestants (limited evidence, side effects); Topical decongestants (only short-term <3 days - rebound congestion); Mucolytics (limited evidence)

questions.explanation: Adjunctive treatments for acute rhinosinusitis: RECOMMENDED: SALINE NASAL IRRIGATION: Physiologic (0.9%) or hypertonic saline. Improves mucociliary clearance. Reduces symptoms. Neti pot, squeeze bottle, or spray. INTRANASAL CORTICOSTEROIDS: Reduce mucosal inflammation. May speed symptom resolution. IDSA recommends as adjunct (especially with history of allergic rhinitis). ANALGESICS/ANTIPYRETICS: NSAIDs or acetaminophen for pain and fever. NOT RECOMMENDED/LIMITED BENEFIT: ANTIHISTAMINES: Dry secretions, impair mucociliary clearance. No proven benefit in acute sinusitis (unless allergic rhinitis component). ORAL DECONGESTANTS: Limited evidence of benefit. Side effects (hypertension, insomnia). TOPICAL DECONGESTANTS (oxymetazoline): Short-term use only (≤3 days). Risk of rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa). MUCOLYTICS (guaifenesin): Limited evidence. Systemic corticosteroids: Generally not recommended for uncomplicated ABRS.

questions.stem: You need to know when to escalate care.
questions.question: What are the complications of acute sinusitis requiring urgent evaluation?

options.text: ORBITAL COMPLICATIONS (most common): Periorbital/orbital cellulitis, Subperiosteal abscess, Orbital abscess; Signs: Periorbital edema/erythema, proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, vision changes; INTRACRANIAL COMPLICATIONS: Meningitis, Epidural/subdural abscess, Brain abscess, Cavernous sinus thrombosis; Signs: Severe headache, altered mental status, focal neuro deficits, high fever; URGENT: CT with contrast if complication suspected → ENT/neurosurgery/ID consultation
Complications are rare and not clinically important

questions.explanation: Complications of acute sinusitis: ORBITAL COMPLICATIONS (Most common suppurative complication): Usually from ethmoid sinusitis (thin lamina papyracea). Chandler Classification: Stage I: Preseptal (periorbital) cellulitis, Stage II: Orbital cellulitis, Stage III: Subperiosteal abscess, Stage IV: Orbital abscess, Stage V: Cavernous sinus thrombosis. RED FLAGS: Periorbital edema/erythema, Proptosis (eye pushed forward), Ophthalmoplegia (limited eye movement), Vision changes/loss, Pain with eye movement. INTRACRANIAL COMPLICATIONS: Frontal sinusitis → Pott's puffy tumor (subperiosteal abscess), Brain abscess, meningitis. Sphenoid sinusitis → Cavernous sinus thrombosis. Signs: Severe headache, High fever, Altered mental status, Focal neurologic deficits, Meningismus. MANAGEMENT: CT with IV contrast (axial and coronal views). Urgent ENT, ophthalmology, neurosurgery, ID consultation. IV antibiotics with CNS penetration. Surgical drainage if abscess present.

questions.stem: This patient has ABRS with penicillin allergy.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: Complete approach: (1) Diagnosis: Meets ABRS criteria - double worsening pattern (improved then worsened with new fever/purulent discharge); (2) No imaging needed - uncomplicated presentation, no red flags for complications; (3) ANTIBIOTIC: Given 'penicillin allergy' (childhood rash) - clarify reaction type; If low-risk rash: Can likely tolerate amoxicillin-clavulanate; If true allergy: DOXYCYCLINE 100mg BID × 5-7 days; (4) ADJUNCTIVE: Saline nasal irrigation, Intranasal corticosteroid (fluticasone), Analgesics PRN; (5) Follow-up: If no improvement in 7 days → reassess, change antibiotic; (6) Return precautions: Periorbital swelling, vision changes, severe headache, altered mental status

questions.explanation: Complete management for this patient: DIAGNOSIS CONFIRMED: Meets ABRS criteria: Double worsening (viral URI improved → then worsened with new fever, purulent discharge). Duration >10 days. No red flags for orbital/intracranial complications (normal eye exam, no neurologic signs). NO IMAGING NEEDED: Uncomplicated ABRS - clinical diagnosis sufficient. PENICILLIN ALLERGY ASSESSMENT: Childhood rash with unclear details - likely low-risk. Options: Clarify reaction (often tolerable), OR use alternative. ANTIBIOTIC SELECTION: If penicillin can be used: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg BID × 5-7 days. If avoiding penicillin: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 5-7 days. Avoid: Azithromycin (high resistance), TMP-SMX. ADJUNCTIVE THERAPY: Saline nasal irrigation (neti pot or spray). Intranasal corticosteroid (fluticasone, mometasone). Analgesics (NSAIDs or acetaminophen) for pain/fever. Avoid antihistamines (dry secretions). FOLLOW-UP: Reassess if not improving in 7 days → consider changing antibiotic. RETURN PRECAUTIONS: Periorbital swelling/redness, vision changes, severe headache, confusion → return immediately.
